tskb.explorer.core.desc: CLI-side explorer layer: graph→chunk transform, HTTP server, and static export

files.name: export.ts
files.desc: Copies pre-built SPA assets to an output directory and writes all chunk JSON files alongside them, producing a fully self-contained static explorer that works offline via file://

files.name: server.ts
files.desc: Node built-in HTTP server that serves the pre-built SPA assets and generates chunk JSON responses on demand. Reads graph.json once, transforms it, and caches all chunk JSON strings in memory for the lifetime of the process.

files.name: transform.ts
files.desc: Converts KnowledgeGraph into ExplorerChunks: a MetaChunk (root, topFolders, docs, flows, terms, externals) plus one FolderChunk per folder that has modules or sub-folders. Two ghost injection passes: buildGhostIntermediaryChains() synthesizes FolderChunks for every filesystem directory that lies between a declared folder's path and its owned modules/subfolders; injectGhostNodes() adds ghost ExplorerNodes for any .ts/.tsx files recorded in folder.children.files that are not declared as modules.

tskb.explorer.app.desc: Vite SPA source for the interactive explorer UI. Built separately from the library (npm run build:explorer) and shipped in dist/explorer/

tskb.explorer.app.components.desc: Node and edge rendering components for the D3 canvas. Each node type implements NodeComponent; edges are drawn by EdgeRenderer

files.name: BoundaryRenderer.ts
files.desc: renderBoundaryGroups() draws a freeform dashed hull outline around all visible nodes that belong to the same architectural boundary. Hierarchy resolution: only the top-level folder carries detail.boundary; all descendants are resolved by walking parentOf. Hull is computed via d3.polygonHull, perturbed with seeded pseudo-random wobble for a stable hand-drawn look, and smoothed with curveBasisClosed.

tskb.explorer.app.graph.desc: SPA data layer: chunk type registry, type-safe loader with LRU cache

files.name: chunk-types.ts
files.desc: Discriminated union types for all chunk shapes (MetaChunk, FolderChunk) plus the ChunkRegistry map and a typed URL builder. Extend ChunkRegistry here to add new chunk kinds.

files.name: loader.ts
files.desc: ChunkLoader: type-safe generic fetch keyed by ChunkKind, LRU-bounded in-memory cache (50 chunks), deduplication of in-flight requests

tskb.explorer.app.layout.desc: Lane layout engine: computes SVG positions for structure (d3.tree), docs, and other lanes

files.name: lane-engine.ts
files.desc: computeLayout(): builds a d3.hierarchy from visible structure nodes, runs d3.tree for left-to-right positioning, then lays docs and other nodes as horizontal lists below. Collapsed folders with _childCount > 0 inject up to 4 ghost placeholder nodes so the tree reserves space for their children.

tskb.explorer.app.store.desc: Pure graph data store (loaded chunks only). UI state lives outside the store in main.ts

files.name: graph-store.ts
files.desc: GraphStore: pure data store holding meta chunk and folder chunks. UI state (expanded set, selected node, search query) lives outside in main.ts

tskb.explorer.app.ui.desc: UI shell components: global spinner, per-node spinner, node hover tooltip, and code preview popup

files.name: CodeTooltip.ts
files.desc: Toggle-based code preview popup for module nodes. Click the <> bubble to show/hide; clicking the same node again dismisses it; clicking outside also dismisses. Renders morphology lines with an inline TypeScript tokenizer (no runtime deps) for syntax highlighting, prepended by import lines with a blank-line separator.

files.name: DocPanel.ts
files.desc: Side panel that slides in from the right when a node is selected or a chip is clicked. show(node) renders the node's HTML content or a structured key-value fallback. showRefs(node, kind, items) renders a docs or flows accordion. wireRefLinks() post-processes every tskb-ref anchor: resolves display text, wires hover to DomTooltip, and triggers setOnNodePrefetch to background-load the containing folder chunk on hover so the tooltip updates with description and correct color once the chunk arrives. Styled by doc-panel.css.

files.name: DomTooltip.ts
files.desc: Lightweight DOM-anchored tooltip used by DocPanel ref links. Positioned above the hovered anchor element (no SVG coordinate math). Separate from NodeTooltip which is SVG-anchored and zoom-aware. mountDomTooltip() appends the element once; showDomTooltip() builds content and repositions; hideDomTooltip() fades out.

files.name: NodeTooltip.ts
files.desc: Hover tooltip anchored to the node's right-center SVG position. Shows a coloured dot, node name, monospace path, and description. Receives updateNodeTooltipTransform() calls from main.ts on every zoom event so it repositions to track the node. Uses mouseover/mouseout bubbling with relatedTarget boundary guards to avoid flicker on child SVG elements.

files.name: Spinner.ts
files.desc: Global and per-node spinner helpers: showGlobalSpinner/hideGlobalSpinner overlay a full-screen loader; showNodeSpinner/removeNodeSpinner attach a small SVG spinner next to a node while its chunk is loading

core.visualization.desc: Graphviz DOT visualization generator - transforms the knowledge graph into a hierarchical diagram

files.name: dot-generator.ts
files.desc: DOT file generator - renders folders as nested subgraphs, modules as ellipses, terms as diamonds, docs as notes, with colored edges for relationship types

library.desc: Refers to the <TSKB> package with all of its capabilities, interfaces and commands

cli.desc: The library exposes a command line interface for main actions - build docs to json graph, generate visualization, search nodes from the graph by query

jsxRuntime.desc: TSKB provides a custom JSX runtime (jsx-runtime module) that transforms TSX documentation syntax into a knowledge graph. Configured via tsconfig's jsxImportSource option, it processes JSX elements differently from React - instead of DOM rendering, it extracts documentation structure, references, and code snippets for analysis and visualization.

tsProgram.desc: TypeScript compiler Program from source files and tsconfig settings that enables static analysis of code structure, types, and symbols without emitting JavaScript output.

registry.desc: The global namespace (declare global { namespace tskb }) containing typed interfaces (Folders, Modules, Exports, Terms) that serve as a type-safe vocabulary registry, enabling autocomplete and type-checked references across all .tskb.tsx documentation files.

graph.desc: Refers to th raw output of the library, where it constructs all the relations and connections between folders, modules, exports, terms, docs, etc. In its raw form it is a json file

dotFile.desc: A Graphviz DOT format file that describes the graph structure with nodes and edges, used for rendering visual diagrams of the architecture

searchResult.desc: Result from searching the knowledge graph - includes ranked matches with scores across the entire graph, found via fuzzy matching on IDs, descriptions, and paths

knowledgeChunk.desc: An on-demand JSON fragment of the knowledge graph. meta.json contains root folder, top-level folder summaries, docs, flows, and terms. folder-{id}.json contains modules, exports, sub-folders, and import edges for one folder.

explorerLane.desc: A horizontal section of the explorer SVG canvas. Three lanes stack vertically: Structure (code hierarchy, d3.tree), Each lane has a labeled background band.

nodeComponent.desc: Interface for rendering a node type in the D3 canvas: enter() appends SVG elements, update() repositions them, anchor() returns edge connection points, getSize() returns bounding box. Implemented by BaseNodeRenderer; override per type.

lruChunkCache.desc: Bounded LRU cache inside ChunkLoader (max 50 entries). Map insertion order is used for O(1) LRU: on get, entry is deleted and re-inserted; on set overflow, oldest key is evicted. Chunks are immutable so no invalidation is needed.

explorerStaticExport.desc: Self-contained static export produced by `tskb explore --export`. Contains the pre-built SPA (index.html + hashed JS chunks) and all graph chunk JSON files. Works offline via file:// because the SPA uses relative fetch paths.

ghostNode.desc: A placeholder ExplorerNode with detail._ghost = 'true'. Ghost nodes represent filesystem directories or files visible in the tree that are not declared in the knowledge graph. Two sources: (1) buildGhostIntermediaryChains() in transform.ts synthesizes ghost FolderChunks for every intermediate filesystem directory between a declared folder's path and its owned modules; (2) injectGhostNodes() in transform.ts adds ghost module/folder nodes from folder.children scanner data. Lane-engine also injects transient ghost placeholders for collapsed folders with _childCount > 0 to pre-reserve tree space. All ghost nodes render as dashed transparent cards with only a filename label.

docs/src/tskb/tests.tskb.tsx.explains: E2E test infrastructure: Vitest suite, fixture project, and CLI validation strategy
docs/src/tskb/tests.tskb.tsx.content:
E2E Test Infrastructure

The test suite ( tests) validates the entire tskb CLI by running it as a subprocess against a realistic fixture project. Tests use Vitest and spawn the built CLI binary via Node's execFileSync, asserting on stdout and generated artifacts.

Full E2E run: global setup builds fixture graph, test files exercise every CLI command, teardown cleans output

  1. vitest.config — Discovers test files, registers global setup
  2. tests.global-setup — Builds fixture graph once via tskb build
  3. tests.helpers — Provides CLI runners and graph loader to all test files
  4. tests.global-setup — Teardown cleans .tskb/ output directory

How the init command is tested: copies fixture to temp dir, runs init, asserts generated files

  1. tests.helpers — Copies fixture src/ into a temp directory
  2. cli.build — Runs tskb init --yes in the temp directory
  3. tests.e2e — Asserts scaffolded files: tsconfig, starter doc, scripts, AI dirs

Fixture Project

The fixture at tests.e2e.fixtureis a small task-management TypeScript app with models, services, API routes, and utilities. It includes its own tskb docs folder with 6 doc files covering all three priority levels (essential, constraint, supplementary) and all registry primitives (Folder, Module, Export, Term, External, Relation, Flow). This makes it a comprehensive test subject for the full build and query pipeline.

Test Structure

Tests are split into focused files under tests.e2e, sharing utilities from tests.helpers. A Vitest global setup ( tests.global-setup) builds the fixture graph once before any test runs.

  • tests.init— scaffolding tests with idempotency checks
  • tests.build— graph output validation: node counts, priorities, edges
  • tests.commands— exercises every query command in plain and JSON modes
  • tests.disambiguation— ambiguous ID resolution across commands
  • tests.graph-integrity— edge consistency, type signatures, external metadata

Running Tests

Tests require the package to be built first (npm run build). Run with npm test (vitest run) or npm run test:watch (vitest) for interactive mode. The 60-second timeout in vitest.configaccounts for CLI subprocess startup cost.

docs/src/tskb/main.tskb.tsx.explains: Architecture, API surface, and usage flow of the TSKB library
docs/src/tskb/main.tskb.tsx.content:
Architecture and implementation docs for the library

The package is located in TSKB.Package.Root, with its package.jsonand npm.README.mdfor npm.

What is ?

A TypeScript DSL for type-safe architectural documentation. Generates queryable knowledge graphs and diagrams with type-checked references.

What it provides in terms of interfaces (API's) and tools

In the root folder: TSKB.Package.Root. In the package.jsonfile, it declares a bin command named 'tskb' that runs the cli

The package exports two main modules, in addition to the cli: the index module Main.index.jsand the jsxRuntime ( jsxRuntime), defined in: Jsx.runtime.js

Core Primitives for Type-Safe Documentation

Main exports (primitives) of the library for creating type-safe architectural documentation:

  • Folder: Represents logical groupings in the codebase (features, layers, packages)
  • Module: Represents concrete code units (classes, services, components)
  • Export: Creates type-safe references to actual code exports using TypeScript's typeof import() syntax - ensures documentation stays in sync with codebase
  • Term: Represents domain concepts, patterns, or terminology
  • File: References non-JS/TS files (README.md, yml configs, etc.) by path
  • ref: A placeholder for referencing anything from the global tskb registry into jsx tags with type assertions

Plus all the JSX documentation components exported from Jsx.runtime.js: Doc, H1, H2, H3, P, List, Li, Snippet - providing a familiar TSX syntax for structured content

A typical flow of usage

  • Create a docs folder in your repository for documentation files
  • Define a tsconfig.json similar to sample.tsconfig.jsonpointing to repo root with jsx: "react-jsx" and jsxImportSource: "tskb"
  • Declare vocabulary (Folders, Modules, Exports, Terms, Files) using global namespace augmentation. Use typeof import() for type-safe references.
  • Write *.tskb.tsx files using JSX components. TypeScript validates all references at compile time.
  • Run cli.build:

    "tskb \"**/*.tskb.tsx\" --tsconfig tsconfig.json"

  • The clicreates a tsProgramand type-checks all documentation files against actual source code
  • Extracts vocabulary from the registry(Folders, Modules, Exports, Terms)
  • Processes *.tskb.tsx files, extracting content and references
  • Constructs the graphby analyzing relationships and hierarchies
  • Outputs JSON knowledge graph optimized for AI agents and tooling

Generating a visual representation:

  • cli.buildautomatically generates a dotFilein .tskb/graph.dot using generateDot
  • generateDottransforms the graphinto a dotFilewith nodes and relationship edges
  • Render with Graphviz: "dot -Tpng .tskb/graph.dot -o .tskb/graph.png"
  • Or use interactive viewers like xdot or online Graphviz tools

Key Benefits

  • Type-checked references: Renames, moves, or API changes break the build - enforcing correctness
  • Validated snippets: Code examples are type-checked against actual source
  • Living documentation: Stays in sync through the compiler - CI catches drift
  • AI-optimized: Queryable knowledge graph reduces hallucination
  • Docs as infrastructure: Compile-time validation, not afterthought

Navigating the knowledge graph

Three commands for exploring and querying the knowledge graph - optimized for AI agents

List all folders

  • Run cli.ls: "tskb ls ./dist/taskflow-graph.json" or "tskb ls ./dist/taskflow-graph.json --depth 2"
  • Always starts from Package.Root, lists all folders hierarchically
  • Controllable depth: default 1 (immediate children), -1 for unlimited
  • Returns flat JSON with folder ID, depth level, description, and path
  • Use for initial orientation and discovering folder IDs

Pick any node

  • Run cli.pick: "tskb pick tskb.cli" (by ID) or "tskb pick packages/tskb/src/cli" (by path)
  • Resolves any node type: folder, module, export, term, or doc
  • Returns type-specific data: parent, children, modules, exports, referencing docs
  • Falls back to nearest parent folder when no exact match is found

Search the graph

  • Run cli.search: "tskb search auth" or "tskb search build command"
  • Fuzzy searches the entire graph across IDs, descriptions, and paths using Fuse.js
  • searchResultincludes: top 10 ranked results with score (0-1, higher = better match)
  • Searches across all node types: folders, modules, exports, terms, and docs
  • Use pick to get full details on any result

Documentation Philosophy: Map, Not Manual

Write structural signposts showing what exists where and how pieces connect - not implementation details.

  • Document structure: Declare folders, locations, and key relationships
  • Use type-safe references: typeof import() ensures TypeScript validates existence and catches renames
  • Focus on WHAT and WHERE, not HOW: Describe purpose and location, not algorithms
  • Trust validation: TSKB catches broken references, not behavior changes
  • Major relationships only: Document architectural connections, not every dependency

The tskb build process: source files through extraction to knowledge graph outputs

  1. cli.build — Orchestrates the pipeline: file discovery, extraction, graph building, output generation
  2. extractRegistry — Scans TypeScript AST for global tskb namespace declarations (Folders, Modules, Exports, Terms)
  3. extractDocs — Receives completed registry; traverses JSX trees to extract content, references, relations, and flows — embeds data-node-display in reference links using registry-resolved paths
  4. buildGraph — Merges registry and docs into a unified graph with nodes, edges, and hierarchy
  5. generateDot — Transforms the graph into DOT format for Graphviz visualization

docs/src/tskb/runtime/runtime.tskb.tsx.explains: Runtime module structure: JSX primitives and registry type definitions
docs/src/tskb/runtime/runtime.tskb.tsx.content:
Runtime

Located in tskb.runtime. Contains registry type definitions and JSX primitives - no actual runtime execution.

Modules

  • runtime.jsx: JSX runtime with jsx.Doc(requires explains prop and optional priority via DocPriority: essential, constraint, or supplementary). Includes heading/paragraph/list components, jsx.Adrfor Architecture Decision Records, and jsx.reffor type-safe registry references.
  • runtime.jsxalso provides jsx.Flowand jsx.Stepfor defining named, ordered sequences through the system. Flows become first-class graph nodes with priority support. Only Step children are allowed inside Flow (validated at build time).
  • runtime.registry: Type definitions for Folder, Module, Export, Term interfaces used in global namespace augmentation.

Purpose

Provides TypeScript types and JSX functions for authoring *.tskb.tsx files. Registry interfaces enable type-safe vocabulary declarations. JSX components structure documentation content.

Semantic Relations

The Relation tag creates a semantic edge in the knowledge graph between two nodes. It is type-safe and supports any registered Folder, Module, Term, or Export. The optional label prop allows you to describe the relationship (e.g., "depends on").

This will emit a related-to edge from the runtime.jsx module to the Graph term in the TSKB graph, with the label "runtime describes graph".

docs/src/tskb/explorer/transform.tskb.ts.explains: Transform: how KnowledgeGraph becomes ExplorerChunks — edge indexing, meta assembly, recursive folder chunking, ghost injection
docs/src/tskb/explorer/transform.tskb.ts.content:
Graph Transform

explorer.transformis the single step between the raw knowledge graph and what the browser receives. explorer.transformGraphis implemented as a class ( GraphToExplorerTransformer ) whose transform() method orchestrates all steps — edge indexing, meta assembly, folder chunk recursion, ghost injection, and child count patching.

Output shape

The output is ExplorerChunks : a MetaChunk plus a Map<folderId, FolderChunk> . The server and exporter serialize each entry to a separate knowledgeChunkJSON file: meta.json and folder-{sanitizedId}.json .

MetaChunk contains: root folder node, top-level folder summaries (with _hasChildren / _childCount hints), all doc nodes, flow nodes, term nodes, external nodes, and cross-lane references edges.

FolderChunk contains: direct sub-folders, direct modules, all exports of those modules, import edges between modules in this folder ( internalEdges ), and import edges that cross the folder boundary ( externalEdges ).

Edge lookup indexes

Before anything else, the transformer builds two Map<nodeId, GraphEdge[]> indexes — one keyed by the source node id ( edgesBySource ) and one by the target node id ( edgesByTarget ). All subsequent queries run in O(1) per node instead of scanning the full edge list.

Recursive folder chunking

buildAllFolderChunksRecursively() does a depth-first walk starting from the root folder, following contains edges. For each folder it finds direct modules (via belongs-to edges pointing at the folder) and direct sub-folders (via contains edges from the folder). Folders with neither are skipped but still recursed into, so deeply nested folders with content are always reached.

Ghost node injection

Ghost injection runs in two passes. First, buildGhostIntermediaryChains() detects path gaps between a declared folder's path and its owned modules or sub-folders. When a module lives at pkg/src/core/server.ts but its declared parent folder has path pkg , the transformer synthesizes ghostNodeFolderChunk entries for every intermediate directory ( pkg/src , pkg/src/core ) and re-parents the module to the deepest one. The chain is not synthesized if any intermediate path is already owned by a declared folder.

Second, injectGhostNodes() scans every declared folder's folder.children.files and folder.children.folders (scanner data recorded during tskb build ). Any .ts / .tsx file not already in a chunk becomes a ghost module node. Any direct child directory not already represented (either as a declared folder or a ghost intermediary) and not a transparent intermediary becomes a ghost folder node. Ghost intermediary chunks are not processed by this pass — they only contain what the graph already knows.

Child count patching

After ghost injection is complete, patchFolderChildCounts() writes the final _hasChildren and _childCount detail fields on every folder ExplorerNode . These fields drive the expand button visibility and badge in the SPA. The patch runs last so ghost nodes are included in the counts.

How KnowledgeGraph becomes ExplorerChunks: edge indexing, meta assembly, recursive folder chunking, ghost node injection, child count patching

  1. cli.commands.explore — Reads graph.json from disk into KnowledgeGraph
  2. explorer.transform — groupEdgesByKey() builds edgesBySource + edgesByTarget indexes from all graph edges
  3. explorer.transformGraph — Assembles MetaChunk: root node, topFolders, docs, flows, terms, externals, cross-lane reference edges
  4. explorer.transformGraph — buildAllFolderChunksRecursively() depth-first: modules, exports, subfolders, internal + external import edges per folder
  5. explorer.transform — buildGhostIntermediaryChains() synthesizes FolderChunks for every intermediate filesystem directory between a declared folder path and its owned modules/subfolders
  6. explorer.transform — injectGhostNodes() adds placeholder ExplorerNodes for undeclared .ts/.tsx files and undeclared child directories recorded in folder.children scanner data
  7. explorer.transform — patchFolderChildCounts() finalizes _hasChildren + _childCount on all folder nodes after ghost injection

docs/src/tskb/explorer/spa.tskb.tsx.explains: Explorer SPA architecture: D3 canvas, chunk loading, three-lane layout, node components, UI shell
docs/src/tskb/explorer/spa.tskb.tsx.content:
Explorer SPA

The SPA is built with Vite (D3 in a separate vendor chunk) and lives in the explorer-app/ directory. It is pre-built into dist/explorer/ and shipped inside the tskb npm package. explorer.spa.mainis the entry point that bootstraps the canvas, wires all interaction handlers, and drives the render loop.

Data layer

explorer.spa.chunk-typesdefines the chunk discriminated union ( MetaChunk | FolderChunk ) and the ChunkRegistry map that keys chunk kinds to their types. Adding a new chunk kind means extending this file only.

explorer.spa.loaderprovides explorer.spa.ChunkLoader: a generic load(kind, ...args) whose return type is inferred from ChunkRegistry[K] . It deduplicates in-flight requests and uses a lruChunkCache(max 50 entries) so folders are not re-fetched on collapse/expand cycles.

explorer.spa.GraphStorein explorer.spa.storeholds only graph data (meta chunk, folder chunks map). Data arrives via loadMeta() and loadFolderChunk() . UI state — the expanded node set, selected node, and search query — lives as plain variables in explorer.spa.main and is passed explicitly to explorer.spa.computeLayout, explorer.spa.renderStructureEdges, and explorer.spa.BaseNodeRenderer. The store exposes subscribe(listener) for reactive re-render.

Three-lane layout

explorer.spa.lane-enginecomputes all SVG positions in a single pass. The canvas has three explorerLanes stacked vertically:

  • Structure — builds a d3.hierarchy from the visible tree (root → expanded folders → modules → exports, including sub-folder nesting). Runs d3.tree().nodeSize() for left-to-right positioning. Collapsed folders with _childCount > 0 inject ghost placeholder nodes so the tree pre-reserves space for their children.
  • Docs — horizontal list ordered by priority (essential → constraint → supplementary).
  • Terms / Flows — horizontal list below docs.

On every expand or collapse, explorer.spa.maincalls explorer.spa.computeLayoutand fully re-renders the D3 selection with enter/update/exit.

Node components

explorer.spa.node-indexis the extension point for node rendering: explorer.spa.createNodeRenderer accepts all interaction callbacks and returns a nodeComponent. Currently always delegates to explorer.spa.BaseNodeRenderer; add a type-dispatch switch here when a node type needs its own renderer. explorer.spa.node-basedefines the nodeComponentinterface and explorer.spa.BaseNodeRenderer— the default renderer for all node types. Each node is an SVG <g> containing a rounded-rect card, type icon, label, description, edge-count badge, a <> code preview bubble (top-center), and a + expand bubble (right edge). ghostNodes render as dashed transparent placeholders showing only their filename.

explorer.spa.edge-rendererderives parent→child links via explorer.spa.buildStructureLinksand draws them as horizontal cubic-bezier SVG paths via explorer.spa.renderStructureEdges. Ghost links (where either endpoint has detail._ghost = 'true' ) render with a dashed stroke and reduced opacity. It also renders the labeled background bands for each lane.

explorer.spa.boundary-rendererdraws architectural boundary outlines. When a folder node carries detail.boundary , all visible descendants (resolved by walking parentOf ) are grouped under that boundary name. explorer.spa.renderBoundaryGroups computes a convex hull over the bounding boxes of all member nodes, perturbs the hull points with a seeded pseudo-random wobble (stable across re-renders), and smooths the result with d3.curveBasisClosed for a hand-drawn look. The outline renders as a violet dashed stroke with a near-transparent fill and a pill label at the top. All elements have pointer-events: none .

UI shell

explorer.spa.spinnerprovides a full-screen global spinner (shown while meta.json loads) and a per-node inline spinner: explorer.spa.showNodeSpinnerappends an animated SVG ring at the node's canvas position; explorer.spa.removeNodeSpinnerremoves it in the finally block after the chunk fetch settles.

explorer.spa.node-tooltipappears on hover, anchored to the node's right-center SVG position. explorer.spa.code-tooltipis a click-toggled code preview popup anchored to the node's top-center SVG position. Both receive updateXxxTransform() calls from explorer.spa.mainon every D3 zoom event so they reposition to track their anchor nodes as the canvas pans and zooms.

explorer.spa.DocPanelin explorer.spa.doc-panelis the detail side panel. Clicking a node calls show(node) , which renders the node's HTML content or a key-value fallback for non-doc nodes. Clicking a docs or flows chip calls showRefs(node, kind, items) , which renders an accordion. The panel header shows the node's relative path in monospace. Ref link anchors ( a.tskb-ref ) inside the body are post-processed by wireRefLinks() : hover shows a explorer.spa.dom-tooltipwith the node's path, type color, and description; if the node's chunk isn't loaded yet, a background prefetch fires via the setOnNodePrefetch callback — the tooltip updates in place when data arrives. Click-through navigation is handled via setOnNodeRef .

User expands a folder node: chunk is fetched, store updated, layout recomputed, SVG re-rendered

  1. explorer.spa.node-base — User clicks + expand button; BaseNodeRenderer fires the onExpand callback
  2. explorer.spa.main — ExplorerApp.onExpand(): calls showNodeSpinner, delegates to loader, calls removeNodeSpinner in finally; adds node id to expanded set
  3. explorer.spa.loader — ChunkLoader.load('folder', id) — returns cached chunk or fetches /chunks/folder-{sanitizedId}.json
  4. explorer.spa.store — store.loadFolderChunk() stores chunk; store notifies subscribers, triggering ExplorerApp.render()
  5. explorer.spa.computeLayout — render() calls computeLayout(store, expanded) — rebuilds d3.hierarchy and recomputes all SVG positions
  6. explorer.spa.buildStructureLinks — render() calls buildStructureLinks() — derives parent→child link list from updated parentId fields
  7. explorer.spa.renderStructureEdges — render() calls renderStructureEdges() — redraws cubic-bezier SVG paths; ghost links rendered dashed
  8. explorer.spa.node-base — render() D3 enter/update/exit — new node cards enter with fade-in, collapsed nodes exit with fade-out

docs/src/tskb/explorer/serve.tskb.tsx.explains: Explorer HTTP server: chunk API, in-memory cache, SPA asset serving, auto-open browser
docs/src/tskb/explorer/serve.tskb.tsx.content:
Explorer Server

explorer.serveExplorerin explorer.serverstarts a Node built-in http server — no external dependency. It runs the graph transform once at startup, serializes every knowledgeChunk to JSON, and keeps all of them in a Map<string, string> for the lifetime of the process. Subsequent chunk requests are pure string writes with no re-serialization.

Request routing

Two routes are handled:

/chunks/*.json — chunk API. The key is extracted from the pathname (e.g. meta or folder-tskb.core ) and looked up in the in-memory cache. A 404 is returned if the key is unknown.

/* (static assets) — SPA files are served from dist/explorer/ , resolved via import.meta.url so the path stays correct after npm pack . Any unknown path falls back to index.html so the SPA router handles client-side navigation. A path traversal guard rejects any request that resolves outside dist/explorer/ .

Command entry point

explorer.explorein cli.commands.explorelocates graph.json via cli.utils.graph-finder, parses it, then branches: if --export is provided it calls exportExplorer ; otherwise it calls explorer.serveExplorer. The module is dynamically imported so the SPA assets directory is only resolved when actually needed.

tskb explore: CLI finds graph, transforms chunks, starts HTTP server, browser loads SPA and fetches chunks on demand

  1. explorer.explore — Reads graph.json, branches on --export flag
  2. explorer.transformGraph — Converts KnowledgeGraph into MetaChunk + FolderChunks, serializes all to JSON strings
  3. explorer.serveExplorer — Starts http server: /chunks/*.json reads from in-memory cache; /* serves SPA assets
  4. explorer.spa.main — ExplorerApp.mount(): setupCanvas (D3 SVG + zoom/pan), setupRenderer, setupSearch, then loadInitialData fetches meta.json
  5. explorer.spa.lane-engine — computeLayout() positions all visible nodes across three lanes
  6. explorer.spa.node-base — D3 enter/update renders node cards into SVG groups

docs/src/tskb/explorer/export.tskb.tsx.explains: Explorer static export: chunk file layout, SPA asset copy, file:// compatibility
docs/src/tskb/explorer/export.tskb.tsx.content:
Explorer Static Export

explorer.exportExplorerin explorer.exportproduces a explorerStaticExport: a directory containing the pre-built SPA ( index.html + hashed JS/CSS assets) and all knowledgeChunkJSON files written to a chunks/ sub-directory.

Output layout

The default output directory is .tskb/explorer/ . The structure mirrors what the server serves dynamically:

index.html , assets/ — Vite-built SPA with content-hashed filenames.

chunks/meta.json — MetaChunk: root, top-level folders, docs, flows, terms, externals.

chunks/folder-{sanitizedId}.json — one FolderChunk per folder with modules or sub-folders.

File:// compatibility

The SPA fetches chunks using relative paths ( ./chunks/meta.json ) rather than absolute server routes. This means the exported output works when opened directly in a browser via file:// without running a server, making it suitable for archiving or sharing as a zip.

tskb explore --export: reads graph.json, transforms chunks in memory, copies pre-built SPA from dist/explorer/ and writes chunk JSON files into the output directory (default: .tskb/explorer/)

  1. explorer.explore — reads .tskb/graph.json; resolves output directory from --export flag (default: .tskb/explorer/)
  2. explorer.transformGraph — converts KnowledgeGraph → MetaChunk + one FolderChunk per folder, held in memory
  3. explorer.exportExplorer — copyDir: recursively copies dist/explorer/ (pre-built SPA inside the tskb package) into outputDir/ — produces outputDir/index.html and outputDir/assets/
  4. explorer.exportExplorer — writes all chunks as JSON: outputDir/chunks/meta.json and outputDir/chunks/folder-{sanitizedId}.json — one file per folder

docs/src/tskb/explorer/explorer.tskb.tsx.explains: tskb Explorer: interactive visual graph browser opened via `tskb explore`. Architecture, data flow, SPA layout, and extension points.
docs/src/tskb/explorer/explorer.tskb.tsx.content:
Explorer

The explorer is a D3-powered SPA that renders the knowledge graph as a visual, interactive diagram. It opens via tskb explore (local HTTP server) or exports to a self-contained static folder via tskb explore --export .

The implementation splits into two layers: the CLI-side layer in tskb.explorer.core(graph transform, HTTP server, static export) and the Vite SPA in tskb.explorer.app(D3 canvas, chunk loading, layout, node components).

CLI layer

The CLI layer has three focused modules — see their dedicated docs for details:

explorer.transform( explorer.transformGraph) — converts a flat KnowledgeGraph into ExplorerChunks : a MetaChunk plus one FolderChunk per folder that has modules or sub-folders. Ghost nodes are injected for filesystem files not declared in the graph.

explorer.server( explorer.serveExplorer) — Node built-in http server that caches all chunk JSON strings in memory after the first transform and serves them at /chunks/*.json . No external server dependency.

explorer.export( explorer.exportExplorer) — copies the pre-built SPA assets to an output directory and writes all chunk JSON files alongside them, producing a fully self-contained static explorer.

Build integration

npm run build:explorer runs Vite, outputting to dist/explorer/ . npm run build:lib runs tsc , outputting to dist/ . The full npm run build runs explorer first, then lib — so the SPA assets are inside dist/ when the package is published.

During development, npm run dev:explorer starts Vite's HMR dev server against a fixed chunks/ directory. The CLI build:lib must be run at least once so the dist/ server code exists.

docs/src/tskb/explorer/app.tskb.tsx.explains: ExplorerApp class: boot sequence, render loop, and interaction handlers
docs/src/tskb/explorer/app.tskb.tsx.content:
ExplorerApp

explorer.spa.ExplorerAppin explorer.spa.mainis the single top-level controller for the explorer SPA. All canvas layers, UI state, and callbacks are owned by the class instance. The only public surface is explorer.spa.ExplorerApp.mount— everything else is private, wired internally via callbacks.

Boot sequence

explorer.spa.ExplorerApp.mountruns four synchronous setup methods then awaits data. explorer.spa.ExplorerApp.setupCanvas creates the SVG layer stack and wires zoom/pan. explorer.spa.ExplorerApp.setupTooltipsmounts hover and code tooltips. explorer.spa.ExplorerApp.setupRendererconstructs the node renderer with bound interaction callbacks. explorer.spa.ExplorerApp.setupSearchwires the search input. explorer.spa.ExplorerApp.loadInitialDatafetches meta.json and calls store.loadMeta() , which notifies the render subscriber and triggers the first explorer.spa.ExplorerApp.render.

Render loop

explorer.spa.ExplorerApp.renderis a pure coordinator: it recomputes the layout when dirty, delegates all application logic to explorer.spa.computeRenderStatein explorer.spa.render-state, then passes the result straight to the D3 rendering functions. No computation happens inside explorer.spa.ExplorerApp.renderitself — the boundary between logic and rendering is explorer.spa.render-state.

Interaction handlers

explorer.spa.ExplorerApp.onExpandhandles folder and module expand/collapse. For folder expand it calls explorer.spa.showNodeSpinner, fetches via the loader, calls explorer.spa.removeNodeSpinnerin finally , then updates the expanded set and calls explorer.spa.ExplorerApp.render. Collapse delegates to explorer.spa.ExplorerApp.collapseDescendantsto recursively clear all descendants from the expanded set.

ExplorerApp.mount(): four setup phases then meta fetch that triggers the first render

  1. explorer.spa.ExplorerApp — new ExplorerApp().mount() called on page load
  2. explorer.spa.ExplorerApp.setupCanvas — Appends zoom-layer → lane-bg-layer / boundary-layer / edge-layer / node-layer; configures d3.zoom with tooltip transform propagation
  3. explorer.spa.ExplorerApp.setupTooltips — mountNodeTooltip() and mountCodeTooltip() attach DOM tooltip elements anchored to the SVG
  4. explorer.spa.ExplorerApp.setupRenderer — createNodeRenderer() wires onExpand / onSelect / onTraceLinks / hasChildren / code preview callbacks to ExplorerApp instance methods
  5. explorer.spa.ExplorerApp.setupSearch — Search input 'input' event updates searchQuery and calls render()
  6. explorer.spa.ExplorerApp.loadInitialData — loader.load('meta') fetches /chunks/meta.json; global spinner shown while pending
  7. explorer.spa.store — store.loadMeta() stores the chunk and notifies the render subscriber
  8. explorer.spa.lane-engine — First render(): computeLayout positions top-folder nodes across the Structure lane

ExplorerApp.render(): layout → edges → D3 enter/update/exit → search dim

  1. explorer.spa.ExplorerApp.render — Triggered by store subscription or direct call from interaction handler
  2. explorer.spa.computeLayout — computeLayout(store, expanded): builds d3.hierarchy from visible tree, runs d3.tree for left-to-right positions. Result cached until layoutDirty is set.
  3. explorer.spa.computeRenderState — computeRenderState(store, layout, searchQuery): derives allNodes, canvasW, structureLinks, relationLinks, matchIds. Pure — no D3.
  4. explorer.spa.edge-renderer — renderLaneBands(): draws labeled background bands for Structure and Externals lanes
  5. explorer.spa.buildStructureLinks — buildStructureLinks(structureNodes): derives parent→child StructureLink pairs from parentId fields
  6. explorer.spa.renderStructureEdges — renderStructureEdges(): redraws cubic-bezier SVG paths; ghost links get dashed stroke
  7. explorer.spa.node-base — D3 enter: new <g.node> elements appended; renderer.enter() initialises rect, icon, label, badge, expand bubble
  8. explorer.spa.node-base — D3 update: renderer.update() repositions and reskins all merged nodes
  9. explorer.spa.node-base — D3 exit: removed nodes fade opacity→0 and translate +8px before removal; search dim sets 0.15 on non-matching nodes

ExplorerApp.onExpand(): fetch chunk if needed, update expanded set, re-render

  1. explorer.spa.node-base — User clicks + button; BaseNodeRenderer fires the onExpand callback
  2. explorer.spa.ExplorerApp.onExpand — If already expanded: collapseDescendants() clears the subtree, calls render()
  3. explorer.spa.showNodeSpinner — If chunk not cached: showNodeSpinner() appends animated SVG ring next to the node
  4. explorer.spa.loader — loader.load('folder', id): cache hit or fetches /chunks/folder-{sanitizedId}.json
  5. explorer.spa.removeNodeSpinner — removeNodeSpinner() called in finally — always removes spinner whether fetch succeeded or failed
  6. explorer.spa.store — store.loadFolderChunk() stores chunk and notifies subscribers
  7. explorer.spa.ExplorerApp.render — render() called after expanded set update — new nodes enter, layout recomputed

docs/src/tskb/core/visualization.tskb.ts.explains: Visualization module: DOT graph generation from the knowledge graph
docs/src/tskb/core/visualization.tskb.ts.content:
Visualization

Located in core.visualization. Contains visualization.dot-generatorwhich provides generateDot. Transforms a KnowledgeGraph into Graphviz DOT format — folders become nested subgraphs, modules/exports are grouped within their folders, terms and docs get separate clusters. Output is written to .tskb/graph.dot during build.

docs/src/tskb/core/registry-extraction.t.explains: Registry extraction: how TypeScript AST is traversed to extract Folders, Modules, Terms, Exports, and module morphology
docs/src/tskb/core/registry-extraction.t.content:
Registry Extraction

extractRegistrywalks the TypeScript AST for registry declarations, then enriches each module with its public API shape via extraction.module-morphology.

How a Module declaration becomes a fully enriched graph node with exports, imports, and type stubs

  1. extractRegistry — collects Module declarations from tskb namespace
  2. extractModuleMorphology — resolves source file, classifies exports, renders code stubs
  3. extractModuleImports — walks import declarations for symbols and paths
  4. graph.builder — assembles enriched ModuleNode with edges

docs/src/tskb/core/graph-builder.tskb.ts.explains: Graph module: data model (types) and construction (builder) of the knowledge graph
docs/src/tskb/core/graph-builder.tskb.ts.content:
Graph Module

Located in core.extraction.graph. Takes extracted registry and documentation, produces a KnowledgeGraph.

Modules

  • graph.types: Defines the graph data model. Node types: FolderNode, ModuleNode, TermNode, ExportNode, FlowNode, DocNode. Edge types: references, belongs-to, contains, related-to, flow-step. Top-level structure: KnowledgeGraphwith nodes, edges, and metadata.
  • graph.builder: buildGraphcreates nodes from registry items and docs, then builds edges — explicit references from docs and inferred hierarchy from folder paths (contains/belongs-to).

docs/src/tskb/core/docs-extraction.tskb..explains: Documentation extraction: JSX AST traversal, reference collection, and HTML content generation
docs/src/tskb/core/docs-extraction.tskb..content:
Documentation Extraction

extraction.documentationis the second extraction pass in the build pipeline. extractDocs receives the TypeScript Program and the extraction.registryoutput, and produces an ExtractedDocfor each .tskb.tsx file. Each result carries the HTML content string, the reference arrays used to build graph edges, and any embedded jsx.Flowand declarations.

Two-pass strategy per file

Each file is processed by extractFromTsxFilein two steps. First, buildRefMap walks all variable declarations and builds the constantReferencesMap— a lookup from local const names like AuthModule to their registry key. This lets the traversal resolve inline {AuthModule} expressions without re-parsing the typeAssertion. Second, JsxExtractoris instantiated with the map and performs depthFirstTraversalover the default export JSX tree, accumulating HTML and references.

Reference resolution

References appear in three forms: explicit tag elements ( , , etc.), inline identifier expressions ( {AuthModule} ), and inline type assertions ( {ref as tskb.Modules['auth']} ). All three routes converge on buildRefLinkvia the refCategoryMapdispatch table inside JsxExtractor.

buildRefLinkembeds data-node-type and data-node-display attributes pre-computed from extraction.registryso the explorer doc panel can render tooltips for unloaded nodes before their chunk is fetched.

Flow and Relation extraction

jsx.Flowelements are validated inside JsxExtractor— only jsx.Step children are allowed, text nodes throw. Each jsx.Stepnode attribute is resolved through constantReferencesMap, then resolveNodeMetalooks up the node type and display string from the registry so every step link carries the same pre-computed attributes as regular refs. Validated flows are pushed into the flows[] array of ExtractedDocand also emitted as an HTML

block.

Single .tskb.tsx file through extraction: constant map → JSX traversal → HTML + references

  1. extractDocs — Iterates Program source files; dispatches matched .tsx files to extractFromTsxFile
  2. extractFromTsxFile — Builds constant ref map, finds default export, instantiates JsxExtractor
  3. buildRefMap — Walks all variable declarations; maps const names → {category, name} via type-assertion parsing
  4. extraction.registry — Passed in as optional param; queried by buildRefLink to pre-compute data-node-display values
  5. JsxExtractor — Depth-first JSX traversal via visitElement dispatch: structural tags → HTML, XxxRef / inline refs → buildRefLink; Flow and Relation elements validated and collected
  6. buildRefLink — Emits <a class=tskb-ref data-node-id data-node-type data-node-display>; pushes id into refs sub-array
  7. resolveNodeMeta — Called per Flow step: probes all registry maps to return {nodeType, display} for the step anchor
  8. ExtractedDoc — Returned: filePath, HTML content, references arrays, flows[], relations[]
  9. graph.builder — Consumes ExtractedDoc: creates DocNode + FlowNode entries and references/flow-step edges

docs/src/tskb/cli/logging.tskb.tsx.explains: CLI logging: stderr-only output, --verbose flag, timing support
docs/src/tskb/cli/logging.tskb.tsx.content:
CLI Logger

cli.utils.loggeris a module-level singleton that routes all CLI output to stderr, keeping stdout clean for query command results.

Design

  • All log output goes to stderr via process.stderr.write — never stdout
  • Configured once at startup in cli.indexvia configure( { verbose } ), driven by the --verbose CLI flag
  • Log functions: info() always prints, verbose() only when --verbose is set, error() for errors, time() prints a label then returns a closure that logs elapsed ms at verbose level

Output Modes

Query commands (search, pick, context, ls, docs) support three output modes on stdout:

  • Default: pretty-printed JSON (human-readable)
  • --optimized: compact JSON (no whitespace, smaller payload)
  • --plain: structured plain text optimized for AI assistants — no JSON overhead, ~30% fewer tokens while preserving all semantic content. Uses plainOut() in the logger

Verbosity

  • Default: cli.commands.buildshows key stages and summary counts. Query commands produce output only on stdout, nothing on stderr
  • --verbose: build adds timing per pipeline stage, path resolution stats, and file paths. Query commands log "Loaded graph from ..." on stderr

docs/src/tskb/cli/cli-overview.tskb.tsx.explains: CLI structure: commands (init, build, search, pick, context, ls, docs, flows) and utils (output generators, content builder, logger)
docs/src/tskb/cli/cli-overview.tskb.tsx.content:
CLI

Located in tskb.cli. Entry point: cli.index— parses arguments, routes to command handlers.

Commands

In tskb.cli.commands:

  • cli.commands.init: Interactive scaffolder — creates docs folder, tsconfig, starter doc, build script
  • cli.commands.build: Full pipeline — files → TypeScript program → extraction → graph → outputs
  • cli.commands.search: Fuzzy search across all node types, returns ranked JSON results
  • cli.commands.pick: Resolve any node by ID or path, returns type-specific context
  • cli.commands.ls: List folder hierarchy with depth control, includes essential docs
  • cli.commands.flows: List or search flows sorted by priority (constraint → essential → supplementary)

Utils

In tskb.cli.utils:

  • cli.utils.content-builder: Produces two markdown bodies — query body and update body (syntax + session triggers) — consumed by skill and instructions generators
  • cli.utils.skill-generator: Generates two Claude Code skills — tskb and tskb-update
  • cli.utils.copilot-instructions: Generates two Copilot instructions — tskb and tskb-update
  • cli.utils.graph-finder: Finds .tskb/graph.json from cwd, used by search/pick/ls
  • cli.utils.logger: Stderr-only logger with info/verbose/error/time — configured once at startup via --verbose flag
  • cli.utils.resolve-node: Resolves any identifier to a graph node via cli.utils.resolve-node.resolveNode(exact ID → path → nearest parent folder). Also provides edge helpers: getNodeEdges, findReferencingDocs, findParent, findAllNodesById.

docs/src/taskflow-app/main.tskb.tsx.explains: TaskFlow application overview: architecture layers, patterns, and tech stack
docs/src/taskflow-app/main.tskb.tsx.content:
TaskFlow Application

Full-stack task management application demonstrating modern web architecture patterns. Three-layer separation: Client(React), Server(Node.js), Shared(TypeScript types).

Architecture Layers

  • Client: React frontend with contextProviderfor state, hooks for data fetching, WebSocket for real-time updates. Details in client-overview.
  • Server: Node.js backend with serviceLayerand repositoryPattern. Controllers, services, repositories, middleware. Details in server-overview.
  • Shared: Type definitions shared across client/server ensuring end-to-end type safety. API contracts, domain types.

Core Patterns

  • repositoryPattern: Abstract data access through repository interfaces. Details in data-layer.
  • serviceLayer: Business logic isolated from HTTP and data layers.
  • contextProvider: React Context for state management instead of Redux. See adr-context-over-redux.
  • jwt: Token-based authentication with refresh flow. Details in authentication-system.

Technology Stack

  • Frontend: React, TypeScript, Vite
  • Backend: Node.js, Express, PostgreSQL
  • Real-time: WebSocket for notifications and live updates
  • Type Safety: Shared TypeScript types across full stack

Domain Models

  • Tasks: Core entity with status, priority, assignments. See task-domain.
  • Projects: Workspace organization with team management. See project-domain.
  • Authentication: User auth with JWT tokens. See auth-domain.
  • Notifications: Real-time updates via WebSocket. See notification-domain.

Documentation Navigation

  • High-Level: architecture-overview for complete architectural picture
  • Client: client-overview for frontend architecture and patterns
  • Server: server-overview for backend layering and request flow
  • Data: data-layer for repository pattern implementation
  • Auth: authentication-system for auth flow and JWT handling
  • Decisions: adr/ folder for architectural decision records
  • Rules: constraints/ folder for enforced architectural constraints

docs/src/taskflow-app/data-layer.tskb.ts.explains: Data layer architecture using the repository pattern for database abstraction
docs/src/taskflow-app/data-layer.tskb.ts.content:
Data Layer Architecture

The data layer implements the repositoryPatternto separate database operations from business logic. This architecture provides clean abstraction and makes the code testable and maintainable.

Repository Pattern

All database access goes through repository classes in Repositories. Repositories provide a clean API for crudOperationswithout exposing database implementation details.

TaskRepository Example

The TaskRepositorydemonstrates the pattern with support for paginationand filtering. It uses PaginationParamsand FilterParamsfrom the shared types to ensure consistency. 

class TaskRepository {
          constructor(private db: Database) {}

          async findByProject(
            projectId: string,
            pagination?: PaginationParams,
            filters?: FilterParams
          ): Promise<{ tasks: Task[]; total: number }> {
            // 1. Build SQL query with filters
            let query = "SELECT * FROM tasks WHERE project_id = ?";
            const params: any[] = [projectId];

            // 2. Apply filters
            if (filters?.status) {
              query += " AND status IN (?)";
              params.push(...filters.status);
            }

            // 3. Apply pagination
            const limit = pagination?.limit || PAGINATION_DEFAULT_LIMIT;
            const offset = ((pagination?.page || 1) - 1) * limit;
            query += " LIMIT ? OFFSET ?";
            params.push(limit, offset);

            // 4. Execute query
            const tasks = await this.db.query<Task>(query, params);
            const total = await this.countByProject(projectId, filters);

            return { tasks, total };
          }

          private async countByProject(projectId: string, filters?: FilterParams): Promise<number> {
            let query = "SELECT COUNT(*) as count FROM tasks WHERE project_id = ?";
            const params: any[] = [projectId];

            if (filters?.status) {
              query += " AND status IN (?)";
              params.push(...filters.status);
            }

            const result = await this.db.query<{ count: number }>(query, params);
            return result[0]?.count || 0;
          }
        }

Repository Benefits

  • Services don't know about SQL - they call clean repository methods
  • Database can be swapped (Postgres → MySQL) by updating repositories only
  • Easy to mock repositories for unit testing services
  • Prevents SQL injection through parameterized queries
  • Centralizes data access logic and validation

Service Layer Integration

Services like TaskServiceuse dependencyInjectionto receive repository instances, keeping the layers decoupled. The service works with the Tasktype and delegates persistence to TaskRepository. 

class TaskService {
          constructor(
            private taskRepository: TaskRepository,
            private notificationService: NotificationService
          ) {}

          async create(taskData: Omit<Task, "id" | "createdAt" | "updatedAt">): Promise<Task> {
            // 1. Business logic validation
            if (!taskData.title || taskData.title.length < 3) {
              throw new Error("Title must be at least 3 characters");
            }

            // 2. Delegate to repository
            const task = await this.taskRepository.create(taskData);

            // 3. Trigger side effects
            if (task.assigneeId) {
              await this.notificationService.sendTaskAssignedNotification(task.id, task.assigneeId);
            }

            return task;
          }
        }

Database Connection

The Databaseclass in Databaseuses pgto manage the connection pool and provides query methods with transaction support. All repositories receive a Database instance via dependencyInjection. 

class Database {
          private pool: any; // Connection pool instance

          async transaction<T>(callback: () => Promise<T>): Promise<T> {
            const client = await this.pool.connect();

            try {
              await client.query("BEGIN");
              const result = await callback();
              await client.query("COMMIT");
              return result;
            } catch (error) {
              await client.query("ROLLBACK");
              throw error;
            } finally {
              client.release();
            }
          }
        }

Pagination Implementation

paginationis implemented consistently across all repositories using shared types from Types. The PaginationParamstype defines the input, and PaginatedResponsewraps the output.

  • PaginationParams defines page number and limit
  • PaginatedResponse includes items, total count, and metadata
  • Default limit is 20 items, maximum is 100 (from constants)
  • Repositories calculate offset from page number: (page - 1) * limit

Data Validation

Type safety is enforced at multiple levels using TypeScript types like Task, User, and Project:

  • TypeScript interfaces define expected data shapes
  • Repositories validate required fields before INSERT/UPDATE
  • Services apply business rules and constraints
  • Controllers use validation middleware before reaching services

docs/src/taskflow-app/authentication-sys.explains: Authentication system: login, registration, JWT tokens, and session management
docs/src/taskflow-app/authentication-sys.content:
Authentication System

The authentication system provides secure user login, registration, and session management using jwttokens. The implementation spans both client and server layers.

Server-Side Authentication

AuthService

The AuthServicein Serviceshandles the core authentication logic including password validation, token generation, and user verification. 

const jwt = require("jsonwebtoken");
        const JWT_SECRET = process.env.JWT_SECRET || "your-secret-key";

        class AuthService {
          constructor(private userRepository: UserRepository) {}

          async login(credentials: LoginCredentials): Promise<AuthResponse> {
            // 1. Validate credentials against database
            const user = await this.userRepository.findByEmail(credentials.email);
            if (!user) throw new Error("User not found");

            // 2. Verify password (assuming user has password field)
            const userPassword = (user as any).password || (user as any).passwordHash;
            const isValid = await this.verifyPassword(credentials.password, userPassword);
            if (!isValid) throw new Error("Invalid credentials");

            // 3. Generate JWT tokens
            const tokens = this.generateTokens(user.id);

            // 4. Return user and tokens
            return { user, tokens };
          }

          private async verifyPassword(password: string, hash: string): Promise<boolean> {
            // Use bcrypt to compare password with hash
            const bcrypt = require("bcrypt");
            return bcrypt.compare(password, hash);
          }

          private generateTokens(userId: string) {
            const accessToken = jwt.sign({ userId }, JWT_SECRET, { expiresIn: "15m" });
            const refreshToken = jwt.sign({ userId }, JWT_SECRET, { expiresIn: "7d" });
            return { accessToken, refreshToken };
          }

          async validateToken(token: string): Promise<User | null> {
            try {
              // Verify JWT signature and expiration
              const payload = jwt.verify(token, JWT_SECRET) as { userId: string };
              return this.userRepository.findById(payload.userId);
            } catch (error) {
              return null;
            }
          }
        }

Authentication Middleware

The AuthMiddlewareprotects API routes by validating jwttokens on each request. It runs as part of the middlewareChain. 

class AuthService {
          constructor(private userRepository: UserRepository) {}
          async validateToken(token: string): Promise<User | null> {
            const jwt = require("jsonwebtoken");
            try {
              const payload = jwt.verify(token, process.env.JWT_SECRET) as { userId: string };
              return this.userRepository.findById(payload.userId);
            } catch {
              return null;
            }
          }
        }

        const authService = new AuthService({} as UserRepository);

        function authenticate() {
          return async (req: any, res: any, next: any) => {
            // 1. Extract token from Authorization header
            const token = req.headers.authorization?.split(" ")[1];

            if (!token) {
              return res.status(401).json({ error: "No token provided" });
            }

            // 2. Validate token
            const user = await authService.validateToken(token);

            // 3. Attach user to request
            if (user) {
              req.user = user;
              next();
            } else {
              res.status(401).json({ error: "Unauthorized" });
            }
          };
        }

Client-Side Authentication

AuthContext

The AuthContextuses the contextProviderpattern to manage authentication state across the React application. 

const AUTH_TOKEN_KEY = "auth_token";
        const AuthContext = createContext<any>(null);

        // Mock auth API service
        const authApiService = {
          login: async (creds: LoginCredentials) => ({
            user: { id: "1", email: creds.email } as User,
            tokens: { accessToken: "token", refreshToken: "refresh" },
          }),
          logout: async () => {},
        };

        function AuthProvider({ children }: { children: React.ReactNode }) {
          const [user, setUser] = useState<User | null>(null);
          const [isAuthenticated, setIsAuthenticated] = useState(false);

          const login = async (email: string, password: string) => {
            const response = await authApiService.login({ email, password });
            setUser(response.user);
            setIsAuthenticated(true);
            // Store tokens in localStorage
            localStorage.setItem(AUTH_TOKEN_KEY, response.tokens.accessToken);
          };

          const logout = async () => {
            await authApiService.logout();
            setUser(null);
            setIsAuthenticated(false);
            localStorage.removeItem(AUTH_TOKEN_KEY);
          };

          return (
            <AuthContext.Provider value={{ user, isAuthenticated, login, logout }}>
              {children}
            </AuthContext.Provider>
          );
        }

Token Storage and Refresh

  • Access tokens are stored in localStorage using AUTH_TOKEN_KEY constant
  • Refresh tokens enable obtaining new access tokens without re-authentication
  • The ApiClientautomatically includes tokens in request headers
  • Token expiration is handled by refreshing or redirecting to login

Security Considerations

  • Passwords are never stored in plain text - only hashed versions
  • JWT secrets should be strong and kept in environment variables
  • Tokens have expiration times to limit exposure if compromised
  • HTTPS should be enforced in production for secure token transmission
  • CORS configuration limits which origins can access the API

docs/src/taskflow-app/architecture-overv.explains: Full architectural overview of TaskFlow: layers, data flow, and type safety
docs/src/taskflow-app/architecture-overv.content:
TaskFlow Architecture Overview

TaskFlow is a full-stack task management application demonstrating modern web architecture patterns. The application follows a clean separation between Client, Server, and Sharedcode.

Core Architecture

Three-Layer Structure

  • Client Layer - React frontend located in Clienthandles user interface, state management via contextProvider, and API communication.
  • Server Layer - Node.js backend in Serverimplements the serviceLayerpattern with controllers, services, and the repositoryPattern.
  • Shared Layer - TypeScript types in Sharedensure type safety across the entire stack.

Backend Architecture

The server follows a layered architecture with clear separation of concerns:

  • Controllers- Handle HTTP requests and responses, delegating business logic to services
  • Services- Implement core business logic using the serviceLayerpattern
  • Repositories- Abstract database operations following the repositoryPattern
  • Middleware- Process requests through a middlewareChainfor auth, validation, and errors

Key Services

The TaskServiceis the central module for task management, supporting crudOperations, status updates, and filtering with pagination. 

class TaskService {
          taskRepository = new TaskRepository(new Database({} as DatabaseConfig));

          async getByProject(
            projectId: string,
            pagination?: PaginationParams,
            filters?: FilterParams
          ): Promise<PaginatedResponse<Task>> {
            // Delegates to repository
            const { tasks, total } = await this.taskRepository.findByProject(
              projectId,
              pagination,
              filters
            );
            const page = pagination?.page || 1;
            const limit = pagination?.limit || 20;
            return {
              items: tasks,
              total,
              page,
              limit,
              totalPages: Math.ceil(total / limit),
            };
          }
        }

Frontend Architecture

The client application uses React with Context API for state management. Key organizational patterns:

  • Pages- Route-level components organized by feature (auth, projects, tasks)
  • Components- Reusable UI components grouped by domain
  • Contexts- Global state providers using contextProviderpattern
  • Hooks- Custom hooks for shared client-side logic
  • ClientServices- API clients for backend communication

State Management

Authentication state is managed by AuthContext, which provides login/logout functionality and user information to all components. The context uses jwttokens for authentication. 

function MyComponent() {
          const { user, login, logout } = useAuth();
          const email = "user@example.com";
          const password = "password123";

          return user ? (
            <div>Welcome, {user.name}</div>
          ) : (
            <button onClick={() => login(email, password)}>Login</button>
          );
        }

Data Flow

The typical request flow through the application:

  • User interacts with React component in Pages
  • Component calls ApiClientto send HTTP request
  • Request passes through AuthMiddlewarefor authentication
  • Controller receives request and calls appropriate service
  • Service implements business logic and calls repository
  • Repository queries database and returns data
  • Response flows back through layers to client
  • Context updates state and React re-renders UI

Type Safety

The Typesfolder contains all shared type definitions, ensuring end-to-end type safety. Types are organized by domain: user.types, task.types, project.types, etc.

Common types like ApiResponse and PaginatedResponse provide consistent structure for API communication.

docs/src/taskflow-app/constraints/constr.explains: Constraint: services must access data only through repositories, never directly
docs/src/taskflow-app/constraints/constr.content:
Constraint: Service Layer Isolation

Services in ServicesMUST NOT directly access the database. All data persistence operations MUST go through repositories in Repositories.

Rationale

  • Maintains separation of concerns between business logic and data access
  • Enables swapping database implementations without changing services
  • Facilitates testing by allowing repository mocking
  • Prevents SQL injection and enforces parameterized queries
  • Centralizes data access patterns and optimizations

Correct Pattern

Services receive repository instances via dependencyInjectionin the constructor.

✅ Good Example:

  • TaskServiceaccepts TaskRepository in constructor
  • Service calls repository.findById() instead of writing SQL
  • Repository handles all database interaction details

❌ Anti-pattern:

  • Service imports Database class directly
  • Service writes raw SQL queries
  • Service accesses database connection pool

Enforcement

  • Code reviews should flag direct database access in services
  • Import linting can prevent importing Database in service files
  • All database operations should be traceable to a repository method

docs/src/taskflow-app/constraints/constr.explains: Constraint: all API endpoints must follow the ApiResponse type structure
docs/src/taskflow-app/constraints/constr.content:
Constraint: API Response Structure

All API endpoints MUST return responses following the ApiResponse type defined in Types. This ensures consistent error handling and response structure across the entire API.

Required Structure 

interface ApiResponse<T> {
          data: T; // Actual response payload
          error?: ApiError; // Error details if request failed
          meta?: ResponseMetadata; // Optional metadata (timestamps, request ID)
        }

Success Response Example 

JSON.stringify({
          data: {
            id: "123",
            title: "Implement login",
            status: "in-progress",
            // ... rest of task data
          },
          meta: {
            timestamp: "2026-01-24T10:30:00Z",
            requestId: "req_abc123",
          },
        });

Error Response Example 

JSON.stringify({
          data: null,
          error: {
            code: "VALIDATION_ERROR",
            message: "Title is required",
            details: {
              field: "title",
              constraint: "minLength",
            },
          },
          meta: {
            timestamp: "2026-01-24T10:30:00Z",
          },
        });

Pagination Responses

List endpoints returning multiple items MUST use PaginatedResponse type for consistency with pagination. 

interface PaginatedResponse<T> {
          items: T[]; // Array of items for current page
          total: number; // Total count across all pages
          page: number; // Current page number
          limit: number; // Items per page
          totalPages: number; // Calculated total pages
        }

Enforcement

  • Controllers must use typed response helpers that enforce structure
  • ErrorHandlermiddleware ensures errors follow ApiResponse format
  • Client ApiClientexpects and validates this structure
  • End-to-end type safety from server through client

Benefits

  • Predictable error handling on client side
  • Consistent metadata for debugging and logging
  • TypeScript catches response structure violations at compile time
  • Easy to add global response transformations

docs/src/taskflow-app/adr/adr-repository.explains: ADR: choosing repository pattern over direct database access or full ORM
docs/src/taskflow-app/adr/adr-repository.content:
ADR: Repository Pattern Over Direct Database Access

Status

Accepted

Context

We needed to decide how the serviceLayerin Servicesshould interact with the database. Options considered:

  • Services write SQL queries directly
  • Use an ORM (e.g., TypeORM, Prisma)
  • Implement the repositoryPattern

Decision

We will implement the repositoryPatternwith repository classes in Repositories. Each domain entity (Task, User, Project) gets its own repository.

Repository Responsibilities

  • Execute database queries (SQL or ORM calls)
  • Map database rows to TypeScript types
  • Handle pagination and filtering
  • Ensure parameterized queries to prevent SQL injection
  • Provide clean API for crudOperations

Service Responsibilities

  • Implement business logic and validation rules
  • Orchestrate operations across multiple repositories
  • Trigger side effects (notifications, logging)
  • Handle transactions when needed

Consequences

Positive

  • Testability: Services can be tested with mocked repositories without a database
  • Separation of Concerns: Business logic (services) decoupled from data access (repositories)
  • Flexibility: Database can be changed by updating repositories only
  • Reusability: Common queries (findById, findAll) centralized in one place
  • Security: Repositories enforce parameterized queries, preventing SQL injection

Negative

  • Boilerplate: More classes and files compared to services writing SQL directly
  • Indirection: One more layer to understand when tracing code
  • Learning Curve: Developers must understand the pattern

Implementation Example

The TaskServiceuses TaskRepositorythrough dependencyInjection. 

class TaskService {
          constructor(private taskRepository: TaskRepository) {}

          async assignTask(taskId: string, assigneeId: string): Promise<Task> {
            // Business logic
            const task = await this.taskRepository.findById(taskId);
            if (!task) throw new Error("Task not found");

            // Delegate persistence to repository
            return this.taskRepository.update(taskId, { assigneeId });
          }
        }

        // Repository handles database details
        class TaskRepository {
          constructor(private db: Database) {}

          async findById(id: string): Promise<Task | null> {
            const query = "SELECT * FROM tasks WHERE id = ?";
            const result = await this.db.query<Task>(query, [id]);
            return result[0] || null;
          }

          async update(id: string, data: Partial<Task>): Promise<Task> {
            const query = "UPDATE tasks SET assignee_id = ? WHERE id = ?";
            await this.db.execute(query, [data.assigneeId, id]);
            const updated = await this.findById(id);
            if (!updated) throw new Error("Task not found after update");
            return updated;
          }
        }

Alternatives Considered

1. Services Write SQL Directly

  • Pro: Fewer classes, less abstraction
  • Con: Business logic mixed with data access
  • Con: Difficult to test without database
  • Con: SQL scattered across codebase

2. Use Full ORM (TypeORM/Prisma)

  • Pro: Less boilerplate, auto-migrations
  • Pro: Type-safe query builders
  • Con: Learning curve for ORM-specific APIs
  • Con: Magic behavior can be hard to debug
  • Con: Performance overhead for simple queries

References

  • Martin Fowler - Patterns of Enterprise Application Architecture
  • Clean Architecture by Robert C. Martin
  • Repository pattern in Domain-Driven Design

docs/src/taskflow-app/adr/adr-context-ov.explains: ADR: choosing React Context API over Redux for state management
docs/src/taskflow-app/adr/adr-context-ov.content:
ADR: React Context Over Redux for State Management

Status

Accepted

Context

The Clientneeded a state management solution for sharing authentication status, current project, and task lists across components.

Options considered:

  • React Context API with hooks
  • Redux with Redux Toolkit
  • MobX
  • Zustand
  • Prop drilling (no state management library)

Decision

Use React Context API with custom hooks, implementing the contextProviderpattern. Create separate contexts in Contextsfor different domains (Auth, Tasks, Projects, Notifications).

Implementation Pattern

The AuthContextdemonstrates the pattern: 

return <CommentForm onSubmit={() => {}} />;
interface AuthContextValue {
          user: User | null;
          isAuthenticated: boolean;
          login: (email: string, password: string) => Promise<void>;
          logout: () => Promise<void>;
        }

        const AuthContext = createContext<AuthContextValue | null>(null);

        // 2. Provider component manages state
        function AuthProvider({ children }: { children: React.ReactNode }) {
          const [user, setUser] = useState<User | null>(null);

          const login = async (email: string, password: string) => {
            // Simulated API call
            const response = await fetch("/api/auth/login", {
              method: "POST",
              body: JSON.stringify({ email, password }),
            });
            const data = await response.json();
            setUser(data.user);
          };

          const logout = async () => {
            await fetch("/api/auth/logout", { method: "POST" });
            setUser(null);
          };

          const isAuthenticated = user !== null;

          return (
            <AuthContext.Provider value={{ user, isAuthenticated, login, logout }}>
              {children}
            </AuthContext.Provider>
          );
        }

        // 3. Custom hook for consuming context
        function useAuth() {
          const context = useContext(AuthContext);
          if (!context) throw new Error("useAuth must be within AuthProvider");
          return context;
        }

Consequences

Positive

  • Built-in: No additional dependencies, uses React's native API
  • Simple: Easy to understand for developers familiar with React hooks
  • Type-safe: Full TypeScript support without additional setup
  • Flexible: Can create multiple contexts for different domains
  • Performance: Components only re-render when their context changes

Negative

  • No DevTools: Unlike Redux, no time-travel debugging out of the box
  • Boilerplate: Each context requires provider, hook, and interface definitions
  • Re-render Optimization: Need to manually optimize with useMemo/useCallback
  • Testing: Need to wrap components with providers in tests

Context Organization

Separate contexts by domain to minimize unnecessary re-renders:

  • AuthContext- User authentication state
  • TaskContext- Current project's tasks
  • ProjectContext- Project list and selection
  • NotificationContext - User notifications and alerts

When to Use Redux Instead

Consider Redux if the application grows to have:

  • Complex state with many interdependent updates
  • Need for time-travel debugging
  • Advanced middleware requirements (sagas, etc.)
  • Large team requiring strict state update patterns
  • Extensive client-side caching needs

Migration Path

If we need Redux later, migration is straightforward:

  • Context interfaces become Redux state slices
  • Context actions become Redux actions/thunks
  • Custom hooks (useAuth, useTask) can wrap Redux hooks
  • Component code remains largely unchanged

References

  • React Context API documentation
  • When to use Context vs Redux - Mark Erikson
  • Application State Management with React - Kent C. Dodds
